title: Data
text:
Overview
The MC2 dataset represents Oceanus as a knowledge graph: entities are nodes, and relationships between them are edges. This page explains what the data contains, how we understood it, and the analytical decisions we made before touching any visualisation.

title: Data
text:
The files








File
Rows
Role in analysis




mc2_journalist_links.csv
2,436
Base table and ground truth. Every link in the complete dataset.


mc2_jounalist_nodes.csv
740
Base nodes table. All entities in the complete dataset. Note: filename is misspelled in the original data.


mc2_FILAH_links.csv
765
FILAH’s submitted evidence. A cherry-picked subset of the journalist file.


mc2_TROUT_links.csv
378
TROUT’s submitted evidence. A different cherry-picked subset of the journalist file.


mc2_FILAH_nodes.csv
396
FILAH’s node list. Fully contained within the journalist nodes. Not used in the main analysis.


mc2_TROUT_nodes.csv
164
TROUT’s node list. Fully contained within the journalist nodes. Not used in the main analysis.


mc2_geo_nodes.csv
2,664
Physical locations in Oceanus with zone classification and coordinates. Used to enrich travel links.


mc2_geo_edges.csv
3,420
Road network between locations. Not used — not relevant to the analytical questions.

title: Data
text:
The graph structure
Each links file contains edges between nodes. Every row is one connection. The critical column is role, which defines what kind of connection it is.









Role
Count (journalist)
What it connects
Analytical use




participant
218
Discussion → Member or Organisation
Primary analysis. Only role that carries sentiment scores.


about
200
Discussion → Topic
Topic coverage analysis


part_of
175
Discussion → Meeting
Meeting structure


plan
73
Topic → Plan document
Output tracking


travel
22
Topic → Geographic location
Travel pattern analysis


refers_to
43
Document → Document
Cross-reference network



Only 218 of 2,436 rows are participant type. Every sentiment analysis in this project is filtered exclusively to this role. Forgetting this filter produces meaningless averages across thousands of NULL sentiment values.
Nodes contain: type, id, name, role, short_topic, lat, lon, zone, city_name, date, start, end.
Node types present: entity.person, entity.organization, meeting, discussion, plan, trip, place, topic.

title: Data
text:
The COOTEFOO board members
Six people sit on the COOTEFOO board. They appear as target values in participant links. Their presence or absence across the three datasets is itself evidence of bias.

FILAH recorded 3 members. TROUT recorded a different 3. Tante Titan — whose full record shows balanced, positive engagement across both industries — was excluded by both groups. Neither set of 3 members is an accident.

title: Data
text:
The critical structural finding
Before building any visualisation, we ran a full overlap analysis on the three links files using composite keys (source + target + role).
Result: 100% of FILAH’s 765 rows exist inside the journalist file. 100% of TROUT’s 378 rows also exist inside the journalist file.
The journalist file is a complete superset. FILAH and TROUT did not collect independent data — they each drew from the same pool of 2,436 records and selectively chose what to submit.



Segment
Count




In FILAH only (not TROUT)
586


In TROUT only (not FILAH)
199


In both FILAH and TROUT
179


In Journalist only (neither group)
1,472


Total unique journalist links
2,436



1,472 records — 60% of the journalist’s dataset — were never shown to anyone by either activist group.

title: Data
section: Understanding what “bias” means in this data
text:
Understanding what “bias” means in this data
The data does not contain a direct “bias” column. Bias must be inferred from patterns. We identified three distinct layers:

Layer 1 — Sentiment bias
participant links carry a sentiment score from -1 (very negative) to +1 (very positive). If one group’s data shows systematically different sentiment toward fishing vs tourism topics, that is evidence of bias in the data they chose to submit.
Verified findings:



Dataset
Fishing sentiment
Tourism sentiment




FILAH
0.209
0.740


TROUT
0.720
0.141


Full Journalist
0.490
0.549



FILAH’s data makes the board look strongly pro-tourism. TROUT’s data makes the board look strongly pro-fishing. The full dataset shows near-equal engagement with both. The gap between each group’s view and reality is the quantified sampling bias.


Layer 2 — Topic omission bias
Two topics are completely absent from one group’s data: - concert (a Tourism topic) — present in TROUT and Journalist, absent from FILAH - seafood_festival (a Fishing topic) — present in FILAH and Journalist, absent from TROUT
These are not gaps from random sampling. FILAH excluded a tourism topic. TROUT excluded a fishing topic. Each group removed the topic that most undermined their accusation.


Layer 3 — Member exclusion bias
Beyond topics, the groups excluded entire people. FILAH omitted Teddy Goldstein (Fishing=0.85, Tourism=-0.50), Ed Helpsford (Fishing=1.0), and Tante Titan (balanced). TROUT omitted Simone Kat (Tourism=0.908), Carol Limpet, and Tante Titan.
The pattern is consistent: each group excluded members whose records would have weakened their narrative and included those whose records strengthened it.

title: Data
section: Data columns we could not use
text:
Data columns we could not use







Column
Problem




industry
Exists in all links files but contains no data in any row. We reconstructed industry classification from the source field using topic prefix matching.


short_topic
Same — structurally present but empty. We derived topic names from source IDs using STARTSWITH() logic against 15 known topic slugs.


sentiment (raw)
Stored as a string. Some rows contain the literal text “NA”. Requires FLOAT() conversion before use.


Geographic coordinates
Available in mc2_geo_nodes.csv (x, y fields). Not used for mapping because the analysis questions focused on zone type and city rather than precise coordinates.

title: Data
section: How we structured the data in Tableau
text:
How we structured the data in Tableau
The journalist links file became the single base table. FILAH and TROUT links were left-joined onto it using a three-field composite key.
Base: mc2_journalist_links.csv (2,436 rows)
  └─ LEFT JOIN mc2_FILAH_links.csv  ON source + target + role
  └─ LEFT JOIN mc2_TROUT_links.csv  ON source + target + role
After the join, rows where FILAH’s source column is NULL were excluded by FILAH. Rows where TROUT’s source column is NULL were excluded by TROUT. Two boolean calculated fields (In FILAH, In TROUT) capture this. A parameter (Dataset View) switches the filter between FILAH, TROUT, and Full Journalist perspectives without requiring separate data sources.
The journalist nodes file was added twice as related tables — once for target node attributes, once for source node attributes. The geo nodes file was added as a third relationship, keyed on target = id, resolving only for travel links where the target is a geographic node ID.
Why not union? Unioning all three links files would duplicate the 765 FILAH rows and 378 TROUT rows that already exist in the journalist file, inflating all counts by up to 3×. The join approach preserves exactly 2,436 rows while making membership flags computable at row level.

title: Data
section: The 15 topics and their industry classification
text:
The 15 topics and their industry classification



Topic
Industry




fish_vacuum
Fishing


deep_fishing_dock
Fishing


new_crane_lomark
Fishing

Fishing


seafood_festival
Fishing

Fishing

Tourism


marine_life_deck
Tourism

heritage_walking_tour
Tourism


concert
Tourism


statue_john_smoth
Other


renaming_park_himark
Other


name_harbor_area
Other


name_inspection_office
Other



Topic classification was not provided in the data. We assigned each topic to an industry category based on the long-form topic description in the nodes file. The Fishing category covers topics directly related to commercial fishing infrastructure and livelihoods. Tourism covers topics related to economic development through visitor activity. Other covers civic/administrative topics that do not clearly belong to either industry. All classifications are arguable — affordable_housing could reasonably be placed in Other, but it is explicitly described in the data as a conflict between fishing workers and short-term rentals, placing it in the Fishing sphere.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
1. Technical Learnings — Data & Tableau

The superset problem is not obvious until you check
The single most consequential technical discovery was that FILAH and TROUT’s datasets are subsets of the journalist file, not independent datasets. This is not stated anywhere in the challenge documentation — it required a programmatic overlap analysis to confirm. The practical consequence was significant: a naive union of all three files would have inflated every count by up to 3× and made all sentiment averages meaningless. Verifying dataset relationships before building anything is not a step to skip.


Tableau relationships vs physical joins: knowing when to break the rules
Tableau’s relationship model is designed for many-to-many safety — it prevents fan-out by keeping tables in separate query contexts. That same isolation makes cross-table row-level calculations impossible. Our boolean membership flags (In FILAH, In TROUT) needed to check null values from two different joined tables simultaneously, which relationships cannot do. Switching to physical joins in the data source’s logical layer solved this, at the cost of giving up Tableau’s automatic fan-out protection. The tradeoff was worth it here because the join keys were verified unique — but this is a decision that requires understanding the data structure first, not a general recommendation.


LOD expressions require clean filter architecture
FIXED LOD expressions compute before dimension filters but after context filters. This distinction matters enormously when building comparison charts. Several charts produced wrong numbers early in the build because In Selected Dataset was a regular filter (blue) rather than a context filter (grey). The fix is mechanical — right-click → Add to Context — but the diagnostic reasoning requires understanding Tableau’s filter order of operations: Extract → Data Source → Context → FIXED LOD → Dimension → Measure.


SUM vs AVG aggregation with colour dimensions
When Industry Names was placed on the Color shelf alongside a Mean Sentiment calculated field (itself defined as AVG()), Tableau stacked the three industry averages into a single accumulated bar rather than showing them side by side. The fix was Stack Marks Off, but the root cause is worth understanding: Tableau computes the mark-level aggregation first (AVG per industry segment), then stacks those aggregations. The result is not wrong by Tableau’s logic — it is just not what was intended. Designing charts with colour dimensions requires thinking explicitly about how aggregations compose.


The raw industry and short_topic columns are a trap
Both columns exist in the links CSVs and appear in Tableau’s Data pane. Both are completely empty. Using either one silently produces all-NULL output with no error message. This is a data quality issue that would not be caught without independently verifying field contents before building charts.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
2. Analytical Learnings

Absence of evidence is evidence
The most powerful analytical findings in this project came from what was not there — the missing concert topic in FILAH, the missing seafood_festival in TROUT, the missing Tante Titan in both datasets. Standard exploratory analysis focuses on what is present. For a bias investigation, systematically looking for absences is equally important. Heatmaps are particularly useful here because blank cells are visually salient in a way that missing rows in a table are not.


Sentiment scores don’t change — dataset composition does
We initially expected to find that a member’s sentiment scores would look different in the partial datasets versus the full dataset. They do not — because each member’s records are exactly the same in the partial dataset as they are in the journalist dataset (since partial datasets are subsets). What changes is which members are present. This is a subtle but important distinction: the bias mechanism is not in the scores, it is in the selection of whose scores to show. Slopegraph-style before/after charts would have been misleading here because the “before” and “after” values for included members are identical.


Individual variation is not board-level bias
Teddy Goldstein’s Tourism sentiment of -0.50 is a real finding. But a board of six with one outlier is not a biased board — it is a board with one member who holds a strong position. Conflating individual member variation with board-level systematic bias is the analytical error both activist groups are exploiting. The summary visualisation of board-wide average sentiment (Fishing=0.490, Tourism=0.549) is important precisely because it contextualises the outlier.


Small sample sizes demand honest presentation
The travel link dataset contains 22 rows. TROUT contributed 4 of them. Expressing this as “TROUT’s industrial zone share was 41% below the journalist baseline” would be statistically irresponsible — that percentage is built on a denominator of 4. We chose to present absolute counts and let the raw numbers speak. The story (TROUT recorded almost no travel evidence at all) is compelling without requiring misleading percentages.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
3. Project & Team Learnings

Validate data structure before any design work
We spent time building dashboard concepts before fully characterising the dataset. The discovery that FILAH and TROUT were subsets of the journalist file — not discovered until a systematic overlap check — required retroactively redesigning the entire data source architecture. Running a thorough data audit (row counts, overlap analysis, field completeness check, type validation) before opening Tableau would have saved time.


The four analytical questions map naturally to dashboard design
Breaking the project into four explicit questions (Q1 through Q4) gave us a clear brief for each dashboard. Charts that could not be traced to one of the four questions were either removed or reconsidered. This discipline helped us avoid building interesting-looking charts that did not contribute to the analytical narrative.


Choosing the right chart type matters more than visual polish
Several early dashboard iterations used chart types that were technically functional but analytically weak for the question being asked. A diverging percentage chart for the travel data looked sophisticated but was misleading at small counts. A stacked bar that summed three industry averages looked reasonable but was arithmetically meaningless. The most impactful improvement to each dashboard came from asking “what is the specific claim this chart needs to support?” rather than “what would look good here?”

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
4. What We Would Improve

Automate the data validation step
The overlap analysis, field completeness check, and topic classification were done manually in Python before building in Tableau. Ideally, this would be codified into a repeatable validation script that could be run whenever the source data changes.


Temporal analysis
The time column was not usable in its raw form due to inconsistent date storage. A proper temporal analysis — did board members’ industry engagement shift over time? did the topics discussed change closer to elections or budget cycles? — would add a dimension the current dashboards do not address.


More explicit null handling in heatmaps
Blank cells in the heatmap currently represent either true zero counts or topics a member never engaged with. These are analytically different: zero means they engaged but the count is zero; NULL means the combination does not exist in the data. Distinguishing these more explicitly in the visualisation (e.g. a grey fill for true zero vs white for absent) would make the heatmap more precise.


Parallel coordinates for member profiling
A parallel coordinates chart with axes for Fishing sentiment, Tourism sentiment, Other sentiment, and total participation count would give a richer member profile view than the current bar chart and heatmap combination. It would also make outliers like Teddy Goldstein and Tante Titan immediately visible as lines that deviate from the cluster.


Dashboard narrative flow
The current dashboards answer the four questions but require the viewer to synthesise the conclusion themselves. Adding explicit conclusion callouts — short text statements summarising what the chart shows before the viewer reads the data — would make the dashboards more useful for a journalistic audience who may not want to do analytical work on top of reading the charts.

title: IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
text: Mini-Challenge 2 presents a visual analytics problem centered on bias, incomplete evidence, and conflicting narratives. Two interest groups, FILAH and TROUT, accuse the COOTEFOO committee of opposing forms of economic bias, yet both rely on partial datasets. This creates an ideal setting for visual analytics because the challenge is not just to inspect data, but to understand how different data samples can produce different stories. Our project helps a journalist explore these competing claims transparently, identify whether bias exists, and distinguish genuine behavioral patterns from distortions caused by missing or selective evidence.

title: IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
text: Mini-Challenge 2 presents a visual analytics problem centered on bias, incomplete evidence, and conflicting narratives. Two interest groups, FILAH and TROUT, accuse the COOTEFOO committee of opposing forms of economic bias, yet both rely on partial datasets. This creates an ideal setting for visual analytics because the challenge is not just to inspect data, but to understand how different data samples can produce different stories. Our project helps a journalist explore these competing claims transparently, identify whether bias exists, and distinguish genuine behavioral patterns from distortions caused by missing or selective evidence.

title: IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
text:
Data
The project uses the MC2 knowledge graph datasets:

FILAH_links.csv
FILAH_nodes.csv
TROUT_links.csv
TROUT_nodes.csv
journalist_links.csv
journalist_nodes.csv
geo_edges.csv
geo_nodes.csv

These are built from two main sources: meeting notes and travel records. The graph contains entities such as people, organisations, places, meetings, discussions, plans, topics, and trips, with attributes including date, time, role, sentiment, industry, latitude, and longitude. These datasets are linked through shared person and place records, allowing comparison between partial and complete views of the same committee activities.

title: IS428 Visual Analytics & Business Intelligence Project
text:
Scope of Work
Our scope covers four main components.

1. Data Preprocessing and Preparation
The CSV datasets (FILAH, TROUT, and journalist datasets) will be preprocessed and designed into relational tables suitable for analysis in Tableau. This includes cleaning of attributes such as dates, locations, and sentiment values, and structuring the data to enable efficient querying and visualization. We will also prepare geospatial data for mapping COOTEFOO members’ travel patterns and meeting locations.


2. Dashboard Design and Development
Interactive Tableau dashboards will be designed to support multiple analytical perspectives. These include dashboards for committee overview, member-level behavior, travel and location patterns, topic and sentiment analysis, and comparisons between the partial datasets (FILAH and TROUT) and the full journalist dataset.


3. Visual Analytical Workflow Development
We will develop visual analytics workflows that allow users to interactively explore the data and trace accusations back to the supporting or missing evidence. These workflows will enable users to examine differences between datasets, investigate individual committee members, and identify patterns that may indicate bias or incomplete information.


4. Analysis and Interpretation of Findings
The final stage will synthesize analytical findings into a clear narrative explaining whether observed bias in COOTEFOO member behavior is genuine, exaggerated, or influenced by sampling limitations. The project will focus on explanation, comparison, and interpretive visual analysis rather than predictive modeling.

title: Results and Analysis
text:
Overview of Findings
The central finding is straightforward: neither FILAH’s nor TROUT’s accusations are fully supported by the complete evidence. Both groups curated their datasets to manufacture a narrative — selecting records that supported their case and quietly omitting those that contradicted it. When viewed through the full journalist dataset, the COOTEFOO board shows near-balanced engagement with both fishing and tourism industries.
What makes this finding analytically interesting is that both groups were working from the same pool of evidence. This was not two independent observers reaching different conclusions. It was two groups with opposing agendas selectively extracting from a shared record.

title: Results and Analysis
section: Q1 — Do FILAH’s and TROUT’s own datasets support their accusations?
text:
Q1 — Do FILAH’s and TROUT’s own datasets support their accusations?
Answer: Yes, superficially — but the support is manufactured through selective inclusion.

What each group’s data shows
When analysed in isolation, both datasets appear to support their respective accusations:

FILAH’s data shows board members engaging with Tourism topics at a mean sentiment of 0.740 versus Fishing topics at only 0.209. This appears to validate FILAH’s claim that the board is biased toward tourism.
TROUT’s data shows the exact inverse — Fishing sentiment of 0.720 versus Tourism sentiment of only 0.141. This appears to validate TROUT’s claim of pro-fishing bias.

These are not small differences. They look like compelling evidence.


The heatmap evidence
The topic coverage heatmap reveals the mechanism. FILAH’s dataset contains zero records for the topic concert — a Tourism topic. TROUT’s dataset contains zero records for seafood_festival — a Fishing topic. Neither omission appears in the other group’s data or the journalist’s data.
These are not gaps from incomplete data collection. FILAH excluded a tourism topic while accusing the board of pro-tourism bias. TROUT excluded a fishing topic while accusing the board of pro-fishing bias. Both groups removed the topic that most weakened their argument.


The Accusations vs Reality chart
Comparing each group’s claimed sentiment values against the full journalist baseline makes the distortion visible:




FILAH claimed
Full reality
Overstatement




Tourism sentiment
0.74
0.55
+0.19


Fishing sentiment (TROUT)
0.72
0.49
+0.23



Each group overstated the apparent bias by approximately 0.2 sentiment points through selective inclusion alone.

title: Results and Analysis
section: Q2 — Is the COOTEFOO board actually biased?
text:
Q2 — Is the COOTEFOO board actually biased?
Answer: No. The full dataset shows near-equal engagement with both industries at the board level.

Board-wide sentiment
Using the complete journalist dataset across all 6 members:

Fishing: mean sentiment = 0.490
Tourism: mean sentiment = 0.549

A difference of 0.059 across a -1 to +1 scale does not constitute meaningful bias. Both industries receive predominantly positive engagement — 43 positive vs 10 negative interactions for Fishing, and 58 positive vs 6 negative for Tourism.


Member-level variation exists — but it is not systemic
Individual members do show different industry orientations:

Teddy Goldstein is the clearest outlier: Fishing=0.85, Tourism=-0.50. He is the only member with negative sentiment toward any industry.
Simone Kat leans tourism: Fishing=0.12, Tourism=0.91. Her low fishing sentiment is the most extreme pro-tourism profile on the board.
Tante Titan is broadly positive across both: Fishing=0.75, Tourism=0.47. Her overall sentiment average (0.82) is the highest on the board.
The remaining three members (Ed Helpsford, Carol Limpet, Seal) all show moderate, balanced engagement with both industries.

The individual variation is real. But it is variation between members, not a systematic board-level lean. The board as a whole is balanced.


Member activity distribution
The member participation stacked bar chart shows how each member’s 174 total participation links are distributed across Fishing, Tourism, and Other topics. No member is exclusively engaged with one industry. Even Teddy Goldstein, whose sentiment toward tourism is negative, still participated in tourism topic discussions — he just engaged critically.
Tante Titan’s profile is notable here: 54 total participation records, more than any other member, with 34 of those in Other (civic) topics. This reflects genuine breadth of engagement — not bias in either direction.

title: Results and Analysis
section: Q3 — Are the accusations weakened by the full dataset?
text:
Q3 — Are the accusations weakened by the full dataset?
Answer: Yes, substantially — for both groups.

What changed and why
The sentiment scores for individual members included in each partial dataset do not change when viewed in the full dataset. The numbers are the same. What changes is who is present and what topics are covered — and this changes the overall picture dramatically.
FILAH’s picture in its own data vs full journalist: FILAH included only Seal, Simone Kat, and Carol Limpet. Adding Teddy Goldstein (Fishing=0.85), Ed Helpsford (Fishing=1.0), and Tante Titan (Fishing=0.75, balanced) to the picture immediately raises the board’s average fishing sentiment from 0.209 toward 0.490. The three members FILAH excluded are precisely the three with the strongest positive fishing engagement.
TROUT’s picture in its own data vs full journalist: TROUT included only Seal, Ed Helpsford, and Teddy Goldstein. Adding Simone Kat (Tourism=0.91), Carol Limpet (Tourism=0.68), and Tante Titan (Tourism=0.47) immediately raises the board’s average tourism sentiment from 0.141 toward 0.549. The members TROUT excluded are precisely those with the strongest positive tourism engagement.


The suppression numbers




Missing from FILAH
Missing from TROUT




Fishing participation links
28
32


Tourism participation links
30
47


Other participation links
38
41



TROUT suppressed more tourism participation links (47) than FILAH did (30). This is the clearest quantification of TROUT’s editorial bias.


The travel evidence
FILAH recorded 15 of 22 travel links. TROUT recorded only 4 of 22. The near-total absence of travel records from TROUT’s dataset is striking — the geographic dimension of board member activity was almost entirely suppressed. Of FILAH’s 15 recorded travel links, 7 were to industrial zones associated with fishing activity, directly contradicting their claim of pro-tourism bias.


Tante Titan — the suppressed member
The most individually striking finding is Tante Titan. She has 54 participation records in the journalist dataset — more than any other member. Her engagement is broadly positive (Fishing=0.75, Tourism=0.47, Other=1.0). Neither FILAH nor TROUT included a single one of her 54 records.
She does not fit either narrative. She is not dramatically pro-tourism (which would support FILAH’s case) nor dramatically pro-fishing (which would support TROUT’s case). Her balanced profile made her inconvenient for both groups, so both groups removed her entirely.

title: Results and Analysis
section: Q4 — How does an individual member’s story change across datasets?
text:
Q4 — How does an individual member’s story change across datasets?
The most demonstrative case: Teddy Goldstein
Teddy appears only in TROUT’s dataset. Switching the Dataset View parameter between TROUT and Full Journalist reveals the following:

In TROUT’s view: Teddy is visible. Fishing=0.85, Tourism=-0.50. He looks like a fishing partisan and provides strong apparent support for TROUT’s accusation.
In FILAH’s view: Teddy does not exist. FILAH recorded none of his 22 participation records.
In Full Journalist: Same values as TROUT (0.85 / -0.50), because his records only appear in TROUT’s selection. The full dataset does not add new Teddy records — it adds other members who counterbalance him.

TROUT chose to show Teddy because his profile fit their narrative. FILAH chose not to show him because his pro-fishing sentiment would have undermined their claim.
The most dramatic absence: Tante Titan
Selecting Tante Titan in the member spotlight and switching to FILAH or TROUT view produces empty charts. No records. Switching to Full Journalist reveals 54 records with a balanced, positive profile. The contrast between the empty panels and the populated full-dataset view is the clearest single illustration of deliberate suppression in the entire project.

title: Results and Analysis
text:
Limitations of Evidence

Sentiment as a proxy
Sentiment scores are assigned in the original dataset and we treat them as given. The scoring methodology is not documented. Whether a 0.5 vs 0.75 distinction is analytically meaningful is uncertain. We use sentiment as a directional indicator, not a precise measurement.


Small sample sizes in some comparisons
The travel link analysis covers only 22 rows total, with TROUT’s contribution being just 4. Percentage-based comparisons at this scale are unreliable — we deliberately chose raw counts for the travel chart for this reason.


Topic classification is interpretive
The assignment of topics to Fishing, Tourism, or Other was made by us based on topic descriptions. affordable_housing in particular could reasonably be classified differently. This classification affects industry-level sentiment averages.


No causal inference
We can show that each group’s data produces a biased picture and that they excluded records that would have moderated their claims. We cannot prove intent — it is possible, if unlikely, that the omissions were accidental. The pattern is consistent enough to be analytically suspicious, but a journalist like Moray would want to treat this as a hypothesis to investigate further rather than a proven conclusion.


Temporal analysis not performed
The links files contain a time column, but dates are stored inconsistently. Timeline analysis was not attempted. It is possible that the pattern of board activity changed over time in ways that would complicate the industry-level averages we report. This is a gap worth noting.

title: About
text:
Here you can find information about the purpose of this project, the technologies used, and the thought process behind our work. 
  
  
    In this website, we discuss more about the project and our thought process. Through the provided data, we have come up with some interesting insights and conclusions.

title: Methodology
text:
1. Problem Framing

What this challenge is
VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 2 presents a bias investigation framed around a fictional coastal town, Oceanus. The town’s oversight board, COOTEFOO, has been accused of economic bias by two opposing activist groups — FILAH (pro-fishing) and TROUT (pro-tourism) — who each submitted datasets as evidence. A journalist, Edwina Darling Moray, has obtained both datasets alongside additional records of her own.
The task is not simply to visualise the data. It is to use visual analytics to determine whether the accusations are supported by evidence, whether that evidence is trustworthy, and what the complete picture looks like once all available records are considered.


Why it matters
This is fundamentally a problem about the integrity of evidence. Both groups make confident accusations. Both groups have data. The question is whether the data was selected honestly or curated to manufacture a narrative. This has direct parallels to real-world government accountability and media bias — making the analytical approach here more than an academic exercise.
The four questions we needed to answer were:








Question




Q1
Do FILAH’s and TROUT’s own datasets support their accusations?


Q2
Using the full journalist dataset, is the COOTEFOO board actually biased?


Q3
Are the accusations strengthened or weakened when compared against the full dataset?


Q4
How does an individual member’s story change depending on which dataset you use?

title: Methodology
text:
2. Data Preparation

From knowledge graph to relational tables
The raw data is structured as a knowledge graph — nodes represent entities (people, organisations, topics, meetings, places, trips) and edges represent connections between them. Each edge has a role attribute describing the type of connection: participant, about, part_of, plan, refers_to, or travel.
For Tableau, this was flattened into relational tables. The journalist links file (2,436 rows) became the base table. FILAH links (765 rows) and TROUT links (378 rows) were left-joined onto this base using a three-field composite key: source + target + role. This is non-trivial — a naive union of all three files produces triple-counting because every FILAH and TROUT row already exists in the journalist file. The join approach preserves exactly 2,436 rows while tagging each row with boolean flags (In FILAH, In TROUT) indicating which groups included it.
The journalist nodes file (740 rows) was added as two separate related tables — Target Nodes and Source Nodes — to enable attribute lookups on both ends of each link. A geo nodes file (2,664 rows) was related separately for geographic enrichment of travel links.


Cleaning and assumptions
Several data quality issues required attention:

The industry and short_topic columns exist in all three links files but contain no data. Topic classification was reconstructed from the source field using STARTSWITH() pattern matching against 15 known topic slugs. Industry classification (Fishing / Tourism / Other) was derived from those topic slugs.
The sentiment column is stored as a string because some rows contain the literal text “NA”. A FLOAT() conversion was applied, returning NULL for non-numeric values.
Sentiment scores only exist on participant role links (218 of 2,436 rows). All sentiment analysis is filtered to this role type.
Geographic node IDs in travel links are numeric strings. A type mismatch with the geo nodes table was a recurring risk and required explicit string type enforcement on both sides of the relationship.



Key structural decision: joins not unions
Using Tableau’s physical layer joins rather than a union was the core architectural decision. Unions would inflate counts and distort all proportional analysis. Joins allow the dataset membership flags to be computed at row level, enabling any chart to simulate the FILAH, TROUT, or Full Journalist perspective through a single filter switch — without maintaining three separate data sources.

title: Methodology
text:
3. Analytical Approach

Overlap analysis
Before building any visualisations, we verified the relationship between the three datasets programmatically. The key finding: all 765 FILAH rows and all 378 TROUT rows are exact subsets of the 2,436 journalist rows. This confirmed that the journalist dataset is a true superset and that both activist groups drew from the same evidence pool. This structural finding is the foundation for every analytical claim in the project.


Sentiment analysis
Sentiment scores (ranging from -1 to +1) on participant links capture how board members engaged with each discussion topic. We computed mean sentiment by industry (Fishing / Tourism / Other) and by member, across each dataset view. The divergence between FILAH and TROUT views — and the convergence toward balance in the full dataset — is the primary quantitative evidence of sampling bias.


Topic coverage analysis
We mapped which of the 15 known discussion topics appeared in each dataset. Two topics were completely absent from one group’s data: concert (a Tourism topic) was excluded by FILAH, and seafood_festival (a Fishing topic) was excluded by TROUT. This selective omission is direct evidence of deliberate curation rather than innocent sampling.


Member inclusion analysis
We examined which board members appeared in each dataset. FILAH recorded only 3 of 6 members. TROUT recorded a different 3 of 6. One member — Tante Titan — appeared in neither group’s dataset, only in the journalist’s additional records. The selection pattern is not random: each group chose the members whose records best supported their narrative.


Geographic / travel analysis
Travel links connect board members’ activities to physical locations with zone classifications (industrial, tourism, residential, government, commercial). FILAH recorded 15 of 22 travel links, predominantly to industrial zones. TROUT recorded only 4 of 22, suppressing nearly all geographic evidence. This geographic dimension provides a behavioural dimension beyond sentiment scores alone.


Comparison framework
All comparisons between datasets use proportions, not raw counts, because dataset sizes differ substantially (FILAH: 765, TROUT: 378, Journalist: 2,436). Every cross-dataset chart uses either normalised values or clearly labelled absolute counts with N displayed.

title: Methodology
text:
4. Visualisation Design Decisions

Chart type rationale








Chart type
Where used
Why




Bar chart (trellis)
Sentiment by industry, three dataset panels
Directly comparable across panels when axes are locked to the same range (-1 to 1). Side-by-side panels make the FILAH/TROUT/Journalist divergence immediately legible.


Heatmap
Topic coverage across datasets
The primary value is making absent cells visible. A heatmap makes zero counts and missing topics visually obvious at a glance — no other chart type achieves this as cleanly.


Diverging stacked bar
Board-wide positive vs negative engagement counts
Answers Q2 directly by showing the ratio of positive to negative interactions per industry. Diverging layout from zero makes the asymmetry visible without requiring numerical literacy.


Dot plot
Member sentiment by industry, per dataset panel
Handles the sparse structure well — some members only appear in one or two datasets, creating NULLs that a bar chart would render as zero rather than absence.


Bar chart (stacked, sorted)
Member participation counts by industry
Shows both volume (total bar length) and composition (colour segments) simultaneously. Sorted by total participation to surface Tante Titan’s unusual profile.


Heatmap (member × topic)
Member engagement across all topics
The richest single chart in the project. Two dimensions (members, topics) with colour encoding sentiment turns a 6×15 matrix into a scannable pattern. Red cells (negative) stand out immediately.


Bar chart (grouped)
Accusations vs Reality
Six bars in two industry groups, comparing partial dataset claims against the full dataset baseline. Horizontal layout allows long label names to be readable.


Bar chart + dot plot (parameter-driven)
Member spotlight
Parameter controls allow the journalist to select any member and dataset view interactively. The bar chart gives industry-level summary; the dot plot beneath gives topic-level detail.


Bar chart (travel destinations)
Travel zone by dataset
Raw counts by destination zone across three panels. Honest representation of a small dataset (22 links) — percentage charts would be misleading at this sample size.





Trade-offs and simplifications
Sentiment as a proxy for bias. Sentiment scores were assigned in the original dataset and we treat them as given. We did not verify the scoring methodology. Sentiment is used as an indicator of engagement quality, not as a definitive measure of bias — a distinction worth noting.
No network visualisation. The data is structured as a knowledge graph and Gephi was mentioned in the project scope. However, for the specific analytical questions posed (sentiment comparison, topic coverage, member inclusion), tabular aggregations in Tableau proved more analytically tractable than network diagrams. A network view would show connectivity but not the quantitative divergence between datasets that is the core finding.
15 topics only. Our topic classification covers the 15 named topics visible in the journalist nodes file. Any discussion nodes that did not match a known topic prefix are classified as NULL and excluded from industry-level analysis. This is a conservative approach — we do not assign uncertain cases to a category.
Tableau relationships vs joins. We chose physical joins for the three links tables rather than Tableau relationships. Relationships would have prevented cross-table row-level calculations — specifically, the boolean membership flags that underpin all dataset comparison charts cannot be computed in a calculated field when the source tables are in different relationship contexts. The join approach trades some of Tableau’s fan-out protection for the ability to compute membership flags at row level.
